title: Chapter 9: The Marcus Face-Slap
content: /* Speed: Explosion | D4+D13 */
Marcus's voice carried through the glass walls of his corner office like a blade through paper.

"No, I don't think you understand. I'm not saying it needs work. I'm saying it's not ready. It may never be ready. Some people just don't have the thing, and that's okay — not everyone has to be an artist."

Wren stood three feet from his desk. He hadn't looked up. He hadn't blinked in her direction. The young woman on his laptop screen — early twenties, dark circles under her eyes, a portfolio clutched in her lap — blinked rapidly, swallowing whatever she'd been about to say.
"You submitted to my gallery on spec. I gave you feedback. You didn't implement it. So now we're here, and I'm telling you what I told you before, which is: this isn't it."
The woman's mouth worked. A small, broken sound came through the speakers. "Mr. Webb, I did implement — I changed the entire color palette, I —"
"Color palette isn't the problem. The problem is you're playing it safe. You're begging instead of demanding. Viewers can smell fear on a canvas, Ms. Chen. It reeks."

Wren's hands curled into fists at her sides. She knew that voice. That measured, clinical cruelty. She'd heard it directed at her a hundred times — in his gallery, at openings, in front of collectors who laughed along because Marcus Webb was powerful and funny and everyone wanted to be in on the joke.
The woman on the screen was crying now. Trying to hide it. Chin down, fingers pressing against her eyelids.
"Take six months. Find your edge. If you still want to submit after that, my door is open." Marcus smiled — that warm, reasonable smile that made you feel crazy for being upset. "We done here?"
Ms. Chen nodded, mutely. The call ended.
Marcus leaned back in his chair. Picked up his coffee. Took a sip. Opened a new browser tab like nothing had happened.
Wren exhaled slowly through her nose.

Her fingers itched. Not for drawing — not yet. For something else. The pen on his desk was a Montblanc, black resin, probably a gift from someone trying to curry favor. She reached out and moved it two inches to the left.

Marcus's hand reached for the pen a moment later, out of muscle memory. His fingers closed on empty desk. He frowned, looked down, saw the pen sitting three inches from where it should be. Picked it up. Shook his head slightly. Went back to his browser.
She moved the folder next.
The red folder — "Q3 Acquisitions" — sat on the right side of his desk. She picked it up, the paper warm from the ambient heat of his laptop, and placed it on the left side, on top of his coaster.

Marcus reached for the folder. Paused. His brow furrowed. He looked at the left side of his desk, then the right, then back at the left. He picked up the folder, stared at the coaster underneath it, and set it back where it originally was.
"Lost my mind," he muttered.
Good start. But not enough.

Wren circled his desk like a shark. She cataloged the room in seconds: the whiteboard with his weekly schedule, the framed reviews on the wall, the coffee mug half-full, the stack of business cards, the small succulent his daughter had apparently given him (a hand-drawn tag read "DADDY'S PLANT" in crayon). She took it all in, and she began to work.
The coffee mug went upside down over the succulent.
The business cards went into the desk drawer.
The framed reviews — all glowing, all featuring Marcus Webb's name in bold — she turned to face the wall.
The coaster went on top of the laptop.

She worked quickly and silently. The pleasure was sharp, electric, almost sexual. Every object she moved felt like a word in a sentence she'd been choking on for years. His space, his precious, curated space — she was rewriting it.

Marcus reached for his coffee. His hand found the coaster on his laptop instead. He looked down. His gaze traveled — upside-down mug, suffocated plant, empty spot where his cards had been. The reviews facing the wall.
His face went through several phases. Confusion. Double-take. A long, frozen moment of processing.
"Hello?" His voice cracked on the second syllable. He stood up, chair rolling back and hitting the window. "Is someone — is someone here?"
Wren stood two feet from him, grinning. A grin that felt like it belonged to someone else. Someone harder.
She picked up his Montblanc pen again and walked to the whiteboard.

His schedule was neatly written: meetings, calls, a lunch reservation at Nobu, a 4 PM skype with "Landing Gallery Berlin." She erased the lunch reservation with the palm of her hand and replaced it with a drawing.
A crude cartoon. Stick figure with an oversized head, tiny hands, standing on a pile of smaller stick figures. A speech bubble coming from the big figure's mouth: "YOU'RE NOT READY."
Below it, she drew a tiny figure flipping the big one off.
She stepped back. Admired her work.
Marcus turned. He saw the whiteboard. The sound that came out of him wasn't a word — it was a raw, animal thing, part scream and part gasp, the sound of a man watching his reality crack open.
"No — no, no, no —"
He stumbled back against his desk. The coaster fell off the laptop. The screen lit up, and in its reflection, he could see his own pale face, the whiteboard behind him, and nothing else.
Wren stood next to him, invisible in every reflection, and whispered: "Viewers can smell fear, Marcus."
He screamed.

Actually screamed — a full-throated yell that echoed off the glass walls. His hands went up, swatting at nothing, at air, at the space where a person should be but wasn't. He knocked his own laptop off the desk and didn't notice.
The door flew open.

Mira — his assistant, early thirties, perpetually tired — rushed in with the urgency of someone who'd heard a sound no office should produce. She stopped in the doorway, taking in the scene: Marcus backed against the window, wild-eyed, hands raised like he was warding off demons. The overturned coffee mug. The displaced objects. The whiteboard with its childlike graffiti.
"Marcus? What happened? Are you okay?"
He pointed at the whiteboard. His mouth opened and closed. "Did you — did you draw that?"
Mira looked at the whiteboard. A beat. Then her face did something complicated — a struggle between concern and the twitch of barely suppressed laughter.
"Draw what? The schedule?"
"The — the *drawing*, Mira. The cartoon. Someone was here. Someone was *in my office*."
She looked around. The office had one door, which she was standing in. The blinds were drawn. No windows open.
"Marcus, there's no one here."
"I *heard* someone. A voice. A woman's voice. She said —" He stopped. Swallowed. "She said something to me."
Mira's expression shifted. Concern won. She stepped closer, lowered her voice. "Do you want me to call someone? Dr. Pham's number is in your —"
"I'm not having a *episode*, Mira. I'm telling you someone was in this room. Look at my desk. Look at the coffee mug. I didn't put it like that."

Mira looked at the mug, upside down on the dead plant. She looked at the reviews facing the wall. She looked at her boss, a man who made grown artists cry for sport, standing in his corner office with the expression of a child who'd seen something in the dark.
"I'll have cleaning come through," she said carefully. "Why don't you take a walk? Get some air."
"I don't need air. I need —" He stopped. Looked at the whiteboard again. The crude cartoon stared back at him, a mirror he couldn't parse. "Just. Close the door."
Mira left. The door clicked shut.
Marcus sat down heavily in his chair. His hands were shaking. He stared at the whiteboard for a long time.
Wren watched him from the other side of the desk.

The grin had faded. Something else was moving in — a feeling she didn't want to name. She'd wanted him scared. He was scared. She'd wanted to take his clean, controlled world and rearrange it into something he couldn't explain. Done.

But watching a man come apart in an empty room, knowing she was the cause — it wasn't the same as the fantasy. In the fantasy, she was satisfied. Righteous. In reality, she felt like something cold had crawled into her chest and curled up.
He looked small. That was the problem. Marcus Webb, the gatekeeper, the crusher of dreams, looked *small*, sitting alone in his violated office, checking the lock on his door twice.
She backed away. Through the glass wall, she could see Mira at her desk, speaking quietly on the phone, glancing toward Marcus's door.
Wren walked out. Down the elevator. Into the street.

The city noise hit her like a wall — horns, footsteps, a jackhammer two blocks over. The smell of exhaust and someone's street-cart falafel. She breathed it in and let it wash over her, trying to shake the feeling of his trembling hands, the sound of his voice cracking.
She'd done what she came to do.
So why did she feel like she'd lost something?
The phone in her pocket buzzed. She pulled it out. A text from an unknown number — the same one from before.
*Stage 3 active. Emotional volatility expected. Don't lose yourself in the noise.*
She stared at it. No sender ID. No callback number. Just the message, clinical and cold, like a doctor's note for a condition she didn't know she had.
She typed back: *Who is this?*
The message sat there. Undelivered. The number didn't exist.

Wren shoved the phone back in her pocket and walked. The late-afternoon sun cut between buildings, painting the sidewalk in strips of gold and shadow. People streamed past her, through her, never seeing her.
Behind her, in a corner office thirty floors up, a man sat alone in a room that would never feel quite right again.
She should feel good about that.
She didn't.
